prompt: In Italia ____ tardi, di solito verso le nove di sera. (cenare)

must_not_include.note: impersonal si has no plural subject to agree with, so it stays singular: si cena

explanation: 'In Italia si cena tardi' means 'In Italy people have dinner late.' To say what people in general do, Italian uses the impersonal si with the verb in the third person singular: si cena. There is no plural subject, so the verb stays singular even though 'people' is plural in English; si cenano is wrong.

prompt: Scusi, ____ fumare qui dentro? (potere)

must_not_include.note: before an infinitive impersonal si still stays singular: si può fumare, not si possono

explanation: 'Si può fumare qui?' means 'Can one smoke in here? / Is smoking allowed here?' Impersonal si takes the third person singular, and a following infinitive (fumare) does not change that: si può. Si possono would wrongly make it plural.

prompt: In biblioteca non ____ parlare ad alta voce. (dovere)

must_not_include.note: impersonal si stays singular even before an infinitive: non si deve

explanation: 'In biblioteca non si deve parlare ad alta voce' means 'In the library one must not speak loudly.' Impersonal si takes the third person singular: non si deve. The infinitive parlare that follows does not pull it into the plural.

prompt: Con il tempo in montagna non ____ mai: porta sempre la giacca. (sapere)

must_not_include.note: non si sa mai is a fixed impersonal expression and stays singular: si sa

explanation: 'Non si sa mai' means 'You never know.' It is a fixed impersonal expression built on si and the third person singular of sapere: si sa. The plural si sanno is never used here.

prompt: Come ____ 'grazie' in tedesco? (dire)

must_not_include.note: impersonal si is singular: come si dice

explanation: 'Come si dice grazie in tedesco?' means 'How do you say thank you in German?' The impersonal si asks what people in general say, with the third person singular: si dice. Si dicono would be wrong.

prompt: In quel paese ____ presto la mattina e ____ tardi la sera. (lavorare / tornare)

must_not_include.note: lavorare is intransitive here, so impersonal si stays singular: si lavora
tornare is intransitive, so impersonal si stays singular: si torna

explanation: 'In quel paese si lavora presto la mattina e si torna tardi la sera' means 'In that town people start work early in the morning and come back late in the evening.' Lavorare and tornare are intransitive, so si is impersonal and both verbs stay third person singular: si lavora, si torna.
examiner_note: Two objectless intransitives, both impersonal (not passivante); split markpoints per criterion 8.

prompt: Quando si è troppo ____, è meglio non mettersi alla guida. (stanco)

must_not_include.note: after impersonal si a predicate adjective is masculine plural: si è stanchi, not stanco
the predicate adjective after impersonal si is masculine plural: stanchi

explanation: 'Quando si è troppo stanchi, e meglio non guidare' means 'When you're too tired, it's better not to drive.' This is the quirk of impersonal si: the verb is singular (si è) but a predicate adjective after it goes masculine plural, because si stands for people in general. So si è stanchi, never si è stanco.

prompt: In vacanza, lontano dagli impegni, si è sempre più ____. (rilassato)

must_not_include.note: a predicate adjective after impersonal si is masculine plural: si è rilassati

explanation: 'In vacanza si è sempre più rilassati' means 'On holiday you're always more relaxed.' A predicate adjective after impersonal si is masculine plural, so si è rilassati, not rilassato.

prompt: Da bambini si è molto più ____ del solito. (curioso)

must_not_include.note: masculine plural after impersonal si: si è curiosi

explanation: 'Da bambini si è più curiosi' means 'As children, one is more curious.' The predicate adjective after impersonal si is masculine plural: si è curiosi.

prompt: Ieri sera a cena ____ di politica per due ore. (parlare)

must_not_include.note: the impersonal si always forms compound tenses with essere: si è parlato, not si ha parlato
parlare is not an essere-verb, so its participle stays singular: si è parlato

explanation: 'Ieri sera si è parlato di politica per due ore' means 'Last night people talked about politics for two hours.' In compound tenses the impersonal si always takes essere, whatever the verb would take on its own: parlare normally uses avere (ho parlato), but impersonally it is si è parlato. The participle stays singular because parlare is not itself an essere-verb.

prompt: Quest'anno in azienda ____ molto ma ____ poco. (lavorare / guadagnare)

must_not_include.note: impersonal compounds take essere: si è lavorato
impersonal compounds take essere: si è guadagnato

explanation: 'Quest'anno si è lavorato molto ma si è guadagnato poco' means 'This year people worked a lot but earned little.' Both verbs normally take avere, but under impersonal si the compound auxiliary is always essere: si è lavorato, si è guadagnato. The participles stay singular because neither is an essere-verb.
examiner_note: Two avere-verbs, both take essere impersonally; split per criterion 8.

prompt: Per colpa del traffico, alla riunione ____ tutti in ritardo. (arrivare)

must_not_include.note: arrivare is an essere-verb, so under impersonal si the participle is masculine plural: si è arrivati
arrivare takes essere, and impersonal compounds take essere anyway: si è arrivati

explanation: 'Alla riunione si è arrivati tutti in ritardo' means 'Everyone arrived late at the meeting.' Arrivare is an essere-verb, so under impersonal si its participle behaves as with a plural subject and goes masculine plural: si è arrivati, not arrivato.

prompt: Dopo la guerra si ____ molto ____ per anni. (essere / povero)

must_not_include.note: essere is itself an essere-verb, so impersonally the participle is plural: si è stati
the predicate adjective after impersonal si is masculine plural: poveri

explanation: 'Dopo la guerra si è stati molto poveri per anni' means 'After the war people were very poor for years.' Two impersonal-si quirks at once: essere is an essere-verb, so its participle is plural (si è stati, not stato), and the predicate adjective is masculine plural (poveri, not povero).
examiner_note: Combines the plural-participle and plural-adjective quirks; two markpoints per criterion 8.

prompt: La domenica, senza sveglia, ____ fino a tardi. (riposarsi)

markpoints.label: ci si with a reflexive verb

must_not_include.note: riposarsi is reflexive, so impersonal si needs the extra ci: ci si riposa
impersonal ci si stays third person singular: ci si riposa

explanation: 'La domenica ci si riposa fino a tardi' means 'On Sundays one rests until late.' When a reflexive verb (riposarsi) is used impersonally, the reflexive si and the impersonal si combine and the first becomes ci: ci si riposa. Plain si riposa would mean one particular person rests; ci si riposano is wrong because it stays singular.
examiner_note: ci si bucketed to impersonal.advanced pending the proposed impersonal.ci_si leaf; external_id prefix si_imp_cisi_ for clean migration.

prompt: Solo con il tempo ____ dei propri errori. (accorgersi)

markpoints.label: ci si with a reflexive verb

must_not_include.note: accorgersi is reflexive, so impersonally it needs ci: ci si accorge
impersonal ci si stays third person singular: ci si accorge

explanation: 'Solo con il tempo ci si accorge dei propri errori' means 'Only with time does one notice one's own mistakes.' Accorgersi is reflexive, so under impersonal si it becomes ci si accorge, third person singular.

prompt: In quel negozio ____ libri usati a poco prezzo. (vendere)

markpoints.label: verb agrees with the plural noun

any_phrases.note: correct Italian, but it sidesteps the si construction the item drills: 'vendono libri' says unnamed people sell them. The si passivante is si vendono.
Very common in speech, but nonstandard: libri is plural, so the verb agrees: si vendono.

explanation: 'In quel negozio si vendono libri usati' means 'Second-hand books are sold in that shop.' With si and a transitive verb, the thing sold is the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees with it: libri is plural, so si vendono. With one thing it drops to singular (si vende una bici). It is the same number instinct as c'è and ci sono.

prompt: In questo negozio ____ una bicicletta da corsa quasi nuova. (vendere)

markpoints.label: verb agrees with the singular noun

must_not_include.note: the noun bicicletta is singular, so the verb is singular: si vende

explanation: 'In questo negozio si vende una bicicletta quasi nuova' means 'A nearly new bicycle is for sale in this shop.' Here the thing sold is singular (una bicicletta), so the verb agrees in the singular: si vende. Only a plural thing makes it si vendono.

prompt: Ancora oggi, in certi paesi dell'Appennino, ____ con cura le antiche tradizioni contadine. (conservare)

markpoints.label: verb agrees with a plural noun set far from the blank

any_phrases.note: Often heard in speech, but nonstandard: le tradizioni is plural, so si conservano.

explanation: 'Si conservano le antiche tradizioni contadine' means 'The old peasant traditions are kept up.' The verb agrees with the thing (le tradizioni, plural), so si conservano, even though the noun sits at the far end of the sentence. Don't be tempted into the singular by the distance.

prompt: D'estate, sulla costa, ____ moltissime case ai turisti. (affittare)

markpoints.label: verb agrees with the plural noun

any_phrases.note: Common in speech, but nonstandard: case is plural, so si affittano.

explanation: 'D'estate si affittano moltissime case ai turisti' means 'In summer lots of houses are rented out to tourists.' The plural thing (case) is the subject, so the verb is plural: si affittano.

prompt: In questa zona ci sono molti vitigni e ____ vini eccellenti. (produrre)

markpoints.label: agreement, cued by the existential earlier in the sentence

any_phrases.note: Often heard, but nonstandard: vini is plural, so si producono.

explanation: 'In questa zona ci sono molti vitigni e si producono vini eccellenti' means 'In this area there are many vines and excellent wines are produced.' The ci sono earlier already agreed with a plural; the si construction does the same: vini is plural, so si producono. Same number instinct, same place learners slip.

prompt: In ottobre, su queste colline, ____ l'uva per il vino nuovo. (raccogliere)

markpoints.label: singular mass noun keeps the verb singular

must_not_include.note: l'uva is a singular mass noun, so the verb stays singular: si raccoglie

explanation: 'In ottobre si raccoglie l'uva' means 'In October the grapes are harvested.' Even though 'grapes' is plural in English, l'uva is grammatically singular in Italian, so the verb agrees in the singular: si raccoglie. The mass noun is the trap.

prompt: I vini bianchi ____ sempre freddi, mai a temperatura ambiente. (servire)

markpoints.label: preverbal plural subject still governs agreement

any_phrases.note: Common in speech, but nonstandard: i vini bianchi is plural, so si servono.

explanation: 'I vini bianchi si servono sempre freddi' means 'White wines are always served chilled.' The subject (i vini bianchi) comes before si but still controls the agreement, so the verb is plural: si servono. Fronting the subject doesn't change the rule.

prompt: In Alto Adige ____ correntemente sia l'italiano sia il tedesco. (parlare)

markpoints.label: two coordinated singulars make a plural subject

any_phrases.note: Heard in speech, but nonstandard: two coordinated languages are a plural subject, so si parlano.

explanation: 'In Alto Adige si parlano sia l'italiano sia il tedesco' means 'In South Tyrol both Italian and German are spoken.' Two things joined by sia...sia make a plural subject, so the verb agrees in the plural: si parlano, even though each language is singular on its own. Compare qui si parla inglese, one language, singular.

prompt: In quella trattoria ____ molto bene e si spende poco. (mangiare)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

must_not_include.note: there is no object here, only the adverb bene, so si is impersonal and stays singular: si mangia

explanation: 'In quella trattoria si mangia bene' means 'You eat well in that trattoria.' There is no object, just the adverb bene, so this is impersonal si and stays singular: si mangia. Learners often over-correct to si mangiano, but there is nothing plural for the verb to agree with.

prompt: In quella trattoria ____ gli spaghetti alla chitarra fatti in casa. (mangiare)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)
correct_form: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

any_phrases.note: Common in speech, but nonstandard: gli spaghetti is a plural object, so si mangiano.

explanation: 'In quella trattoria si mangiano gli spaghetti fatti in casa' means 'In that trattoria they eat home-made spaghetti.' Now there is a plural object (gli spaghetti) which is the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees in the plural: si mangiano. Compare si mangia bene, where there is no object at all.

prompt: Al mare, d'estate, ____ decisamente meglio che in città. (vivere)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

must_not_include.note: vivere is intransitive with no object here, so si is impersonal and singular: si vive

explanation: 'Al mare si vive meglio' means 'You live better at the seaside.' Vivere here is intransitive with no object, so si is impersonal and singular: si vive. There is nothing for it to agree with.

prompt: In questo ristorante ____ soltanto prodotti del territorio. (usare)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)
correct_form: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

any_phrases.note: Often heard, but nonstandard: prodotti is plural, so si usano.

explanation: 'In questo ristorante si usano soltanto prodotti del territorio' means 'In this restaurant they use only local produce.' Prodotti is a plural object and so the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees in the plural: si usano.

prompt: Con questo caldo, la notte non ____ bene. (dormire)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

must_not_include.note: dormire is intransitive, so si is impersonal and singular: si dorme

explanation: 'Con questo caldo non si dorme bene' means 'You don't sleep well in this heat.' Dormire is intransitive, so si is impersonal and stays singular: si dorme.

prompt: Prima di una gara ____ sempre gli stessi gesti scaramantici. (ripetere)
candidate_forms: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)
correct_form: si passivante (verb agrees with the noun)

any_phrases.note: Common in speech, but nonstandard: gli stessi gesti is plural, so si ripetono.

explanation: 'Prima di una gara si ripetono sempre gli stessi gesti' means 'Before a race people always repeat the same rituals.' Gli stessi gesti is a plural object and therefore the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees in the plural: si ripetono.

prompt: You see this sign in a shop window: 'Vendesi'. What does it tell you?
choices: The shop or item is for sale
The shop is looking for staff

markpoints.label: reading Vendesi
any_phrases: The shop or item is for sale

must_not_include.phrase: The shop is looking for staff

explanation: 'Vendesi' means 'For sale.' On signs and in small ads, Italian glues si onto the end of the verb and inverts the order: si vende becomes vendesi. So Vendesi on a window means the shop or the item is for sale.

prompt: A bar window reads 'Cercasi cameriere'. What does it mean?
explanation: 'Cercasi cameriere' means 'Waiter wanted.' Cercasi is si cerca ('one is looking for') written as a sign, with si attached to the verb. It is how Italian small ads say that something or someone is being sought.

prompt: Rewrite as a small-ad sign, attaching si to the verb: 'Si affitta un appartamento' becomes '____ appartamento.'

must_not_include.note: on a sign the si attaches to the end of the verb: affittasi

explanation: 'Affittasi appartamento' means 'Flat for rent.' In the sign register, si is attached to the end of the verb and the order inverts: si affitta becomes affittasi. The meaning is the same as si affitta, just the frozen small-ad form.

prompt: A traditional small ad offering rooms to let usually reads '____ camere', in the frozen sign form. (affittare)

markpoints.label: frozen sign form, usually singular even with a plural noun

must_not_include.note: the sign register glues si to the verb; the usual frozen form stays singular: affittasi camere

explanation: 'Affittasi camere' means 'Rooms to let.' The sign register attaches si to the verb, and the frozen form normally stays singular even when what follows is plural: affittasi camere. The older, fully agreeing affittansi camere also exists and is still seen, but the singular affittasi is the usual modern sign.
examiner_note: Graded any_phrases: affittansi (older agreeing form) at 0.8, a form-variant like visto/veduto, not the contested si-vende agreement graded credit.

prompt: In 'Qui si lavora troppo', what does the si mean?
explanation: 'Qui si lavora troppo' means 'People work too much here.' No one is doing something to themselves, so this si is impersonal (one, people in general), not the reflexive 'himself'. Reading it as reflexive ('works himself') is the classic slip.

prompt: In 'Marco si alza sempre presto', what does the si mean?
explanation: 'Marco si alza sempre presto' means 'Marco always gets up early.' There is a clear subject (Marco) acting on himself, so si is reflexive: alzarsi, to get oneself up. Compare qui si lavora, where there is no subject and si is impersonal.

prompt: 'Questi maglioni delicati si lavano a mano.' Here si lavano means:
explanation: 'Questi maglioni si lavano a mano' means 'These jumpers are washed by hand.' Jumpers can't wash themselves, so the reflexive reading is impossible and si is passivante: they are washed. The same string si lavano is reflexive with a human subject (i bambini si lavano, the children wash themselves); context decides.

prompt: Which spelling is correct for the word meaning 'yes'?
explanation: The word meaning 'yes' is always written with an accent: sì. Without the accent, si is the little pronoun (reflexive, impersonal or passivante), as in si mangia bene. The accent is what tells the two apart in writing: 'Sì, si può' means 'Yes, one can.'
